revision_reviewed: rev-5 (rev-4 fix set applied, plan as of 2026-05-12 uncommitted edits)
narrative:
Rev-4 is a genuine structural inversion of the B1 pattern, not a filter-relocation. The core diagnosis: the rev-2 'raw_value not in json.dumps(redacted)' shape and the rev-3 'isinstance(raw_value, str) and raw_value' filter both failed for the same reason — they were intrinsically string-typed comparisons that could not operate on non-string Sensitive[T]. The rev-4 `!=` comparison uses Python __ne__, which is type-agnostic; there is no equivalent filter that could be silently re-introduced at the comparison site to exempt an annotation class. The structural failure mode from three prior passes (a gate that admits a known annotation class without comparison) is genuinely closed.

The residual surface is the empty-container vacuous-pass at container-descent paths ([*] or {*} segments). When `raw_pairs = dict(raw_extracted)` is `{}`, the key-equality assertion passes trivially and the per-key loop never runs. The plan's defense ('other examples will exercise non-empty cases, max_examples=50') is empirical rather than mechanical. However, this surface is conditional on a Sensitive marker appearing on a container sub-element — e.g. `list[Annotated[str, Sensitive()]]` or `dict[str, Annotated[str, Sensitive()]]`. All Sensitive annotations in the current plan scope use the marker at the field level: `Annotated[str, Sensitive(...)]` or `Annotated[dict[str, Any], Sensitive(summarizer=...)]` — both are scalar-path comparisons in the test (the latter takes the scalar branch because the Sensitive marker is on the field, not on an element of the dict value). The container-descent branch is unreachable given the current MANIFEST shapes. The empty-container warning therefore describes a theoretical future risk (new MANIFEST entries with sub-element Sensitive markers) rather than a present gap.

Mutation-in-place is not a silent-pass risk: if `redact_tool_call_arguments` mutates raw_args in place and returns it, both value_provider calls see the redacted dict and `!=` fails loudly. If it returns a copy, raw_args is untouched. No vacuous-pass mode.

Type coercion via `model_dump()` is also not an asymmetry risk: both raw_args and redacted_args are derived from the same Pydantic serialisation path; any coercion is symmetric and the != comparison sees equivalent shapes.

M1 (unconditional _TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS removal) and M2 (MissingRequiredPaths grep sweep) are both genuinely closed. The wave-by-wave procedure is sound: Task 4 updates set_source matches, Tasks 13/14 run the same grep on a smaller residual set (set_source entries already removed), and each task explicitly scopes 'leave matches for tools not promoted yet in this Wave.'

The Done-When list (22 items) is manually enforced via checkboxes. CLAUDE.md's 'if a constraint isn't mechanically enforced, assume the next session won't know about it' applies here. No CI step fails if the list is not all-green at merge. This is the same governance posture as Phase 1B and 1C (which shipped successfully), and the operator owns the merge decision — but the risk should be documented rather than promoted to a blocker.

The bulk-editorial PR saturation risk (N6/S5) remains a documented known limitation. No new mechanical mitigation exists. The operator is currently the only PR author, which bounds the practical risk, but the systemic shape is unchanged.

warnings.title: Container-descent branch admits vacuous-pass if Hypothesis generates an empty container
warnings.issue:
When raw_pairs = {} (Hypothesis-generated empty dict or list at a [*] or {*} path), the key-equality assertion passes trivially and the per-key loop never executes. No assertion fires for that example. The plan's defense is 'max_examples=50 will generate non-empty cases empirically.' This is probabilistic, not mechanical.

Current impact: LOW. All Sensitive annotations in the current MANIFEST scope are at field level (Annotated[str, Sensitive(...)] or Annotated[dict[str, Any], Sensitive(summarizer=...)]). These are scalar-path comparisons in the test; the container-descent branch is only reached by sub-element markers (list[Annotated[str, Sensitive()]] or dict[str, Annotated[str, Sensitive()]]). No current or planned tool uses sub-element Sensitive markers. Container-descent branch is unreachable today.

Future risk: if a MANIFEST entry is added with a sub-element Sensitive marker, the empty-container vacuous-pass becomes live. Mitigation: add a Hypothesis event() or assume() annotation asserting non-empty when the container-descent branch is entered, or add an explicit st.assume(len(raw_pairs) > 0) in the inner branch. This would close the gap without blocking the test on empty examples.
warnings.severity: Warning — theoretical for current MANIFEST scope, real if sub-element Sensitive markers are introduced later

warnings.title: Done-When 22-item checklist is manually enforced via checkboxes, not CI
warnings.issue:
The Phase 2 Done-When list is a human-curated checkbox list. No CI step fails if one or more items are unchecked at PR merge. CLAUDE.md: 'if a constraint isn't mechanically enforced, assume the next session won't know about it.' A future implementer who opens the PR without verifying all Done-When items could land a partial Phase 2. This is the same posture as Phase 1B and 1C, which shipped successfully, so the risk is bounded in practice — but the systemic gap exists.

Recommendation: add a test_phase2_done_when.py meta-test that imports each of the explicitly named test files from the Done-When list and asserts their existence, closing the purely-mechanical subset of the checklist.
warnings.severity: Warning — same governance posture as prior phases; no new regression introduced

warnings.title: Bulk-editorial PR clears arbitrary number of simultaneous weakenings with single label-gate check
warnings.issue: The direction-aware label gate (Task 18) sums sensitive_path_count across all changed entries but clears them with a single 'policy-weaken-justified' label and single PR-body rationale section. A PR that weakens 12+ tools' Sensitive coverage simultaneously passes with the same governance overhead as a single-tool weakening. This is the S5/N6 documented limitation from rev-4 Appendix D. The operator is currently the sole PR author, which bounds the practical risk, but the structural gap is unchanged from rev-4.
warnings.severity: Warning — documented known limitation, operator-bounded in practice

warnings.title: Phase 3 has no independent integration test at the persistence boundary to catch a still-vacuous property test
warnings.issue: The property test is the primary claim that underpins Phase 3's redaction correctness assumption. If the property test were somehow vacuous despite rev-4 hardening, Phase 3 would build on an unchecked claim. Phase 3 does not yet have a planned integration test that independently verifies end-to-end redaction at the persistence surface (chat_messages / JSONL sidecar). The tracer-bullet test (Task 6 for set_source) provides one independent check, but it covers only set_source. Phase 3's implementation plan should include at least one end-to-end test that drives a non-set_source promoted tool through the compose loop → redact → persist path and asserts the raw value is absent from the persisted payload. This is a gap in Phase 3's scope, not a Phase 2 blocker.
warnings.severity: Warning — Phase 3 planning risk, not a Phase 2 blocker

minor.title: _TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS removal conditional language removed from Task 15
minor.issue: Task 15 (patch_*_options promotion) uses the unconditional removal pattern ('promote to a Pydantic argument model where options is Annotated[dict[str, Any], Sensitive(summarizer=...)]') but the Task 15 step text says 'Determine whether their options dicts can carry secret-shaped values' with three outcomes, one of which (outcome 2) is to leave the tool as declarative rather than type-driven. For a declarative entry, _TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS removal would still need to happen (if it exists for that tool) without a Pydantic model replacing it. The task should be explicit: whether or not an entry is promoted, _TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS removal is unconditional for any tool handled in Task 15.
minor.status: Minor — the three-outcomes investigation step is reasonable, but the removal rule should be stated unconditionally to prevent a re-introduction of the two-checks pattern for declarative entries

minor.title: m_dynamic_tool_name closed in Appendix D but no assertion exists in any task step
minor.issue: Appendix D entry rev-4 S4/N5 closes m_dynamic_tool_name with reference to spec rev-5 §4.2.2 covering static-name discipline. No task step adds a mechanical assertion (e.g. a test that MANIFEST keys are all literal str values present at module load, not closures or computed strings). The closure is documentary. Given the advisory note ('no further code change needed'), this is a minor carry-over but worth noting for the next session reviewing the MANIFEST construction.
minor.status: Minor — documentary closure is consistent with prior treatment; low risk

B1_property_test_rewrite.pattern_assessment: Rev-4 is a genuine structural inversion, not the same shape with the next filter removed. The analysis: rev-2 and rev-3 shared a common fault — they were intrinsically string-typed comparisons (substring containment and str equality with an isinstance gate). Both required strings on both sides of the comparison and therefore could not operate on Sensitive[T] where T is dict, bytes, int, bool, or empty-string. The isinstance filter in rev-3 was not an arbitrary add-on; it was load-bearing for the substring comparison semantics. Rev-4 replaces the substring/serialization approach with type-agnostic Python __ne__ at the path level. __ne__ is defined for every Python object; there is no equivalent filter that could be silently re-inserted to exempt an annotation class. The B1 failure shape — a comparison that is intrinsically typed and silently exempts known annotation classes — has no reintroduction surface in rev-4.

residual_silent_pass_modes.current_reachability: UNREACHABLE under current MANIFEST shapes (all Sensitive markers are at field level, not sub-element level; container-descent branch requires [*] or {*} path which only exists for sub-element markers)
residual_silent_pass_modes.future_risk: if sub-element Sensitive markers are introduced, empty-container becomes a real vacuous-pass; mitigation is st.assume(len(raw_pairs) > 0) or Hypothesis event()

B1_property_test_rewrite.evidence: All Sensitive annotations in the plan scope are field-level: Annotated[str, Sensitive(...)], Annotated[bytes, Sensitive(...)], Annotated[dict[str, Any], Sensitive(summarizer=...)]. These all take the scalar branch in the test (line 1907: 'else: Scalar descent'). The container-descent branch at line 1869 is entered only for [*] or {*} paths, which are produced only when walk_model_schema descends into a container whose element type carries a Sensitive marker. No tool in the current or planned MANIFEST uses that shape. Grep of 'list\[.*Sensitive\|dict.*Sensitive.*sub' yields zero matches for sub-element shapes across the plan's Pydantic model sketches.

M1_unconditional_removal_idempotency.evidence: Task 4 Step 4 states 'REMOVE _TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS["set_source"] unconditionally in the same commit.' Task 13 states 'REMOVE unconditionally' and cites Task 4 rationale. Task 14 is 'Same shape as Task 13 including the unconditional _TOOL_REQUIRED_PATHS[tool] removal per Task 4 M1.' Appendix D row rev-4 M1 confirms: 'Tasks 4, 13, 14 rewritten: removal is mandatory (not conditional on a redundancy check); rationale cites CLAUDE.md No Legacy Code Policy.' The conditional phrasing from rev-4 systems S6/N1 is gone. Task 15 has a minor residual (see N1 above) but it does not reintroduce conditionality for tasks 4/13/14.

M2_grep_step_wave_by_wave.evidence: Task 4 Step 4 contains the canonical grep procedure ('grep -Rn "MissingRequiredPaths" tests/ src/elspeth/web/ --include="*.py"' with per-match decision rules). Tasks 13 and 14 each cite 'the per-tool MissingRequiredPaths test-pin update per Task 4' with explicit per-tool reminders. Done-When item at line 2051 requires the grep to show zero set_source/create_blob/update_blob/set_source_from_blob/set_pipeline/apply_pipeline_recipe matches in tests/. The wave-by-wave execution is sound: Task 4 removes set_source matches; when Task 13 runs the grep it finds fewer matches and only acts on its tool's matches. No match-removal by Task 4 would leave a Task 13 match unexpectedly absent — Task 13 acts on 'any match whose test scenario invokes THIS tool's handler' which is unaffected by Task 4 having already removed the set_source match.

dependency_chain_assessment.phase_3_inheritance: Phase 3 inherits the redaction-correctness claim from Phase 2's property test. The claim chain is: property test (Task 19) → Done-When item → Phase 3 precondition listed at lines 2021-2025. If the property test were vacuously passing, Phase 3 would build on a false assurance. The tracer-bullet test (Task 6) provides one independent verification for set_source, but not for the patch_*_options wave (Task 15) or blob tools (Tasks 13/14). The missing independent check at the Phase 3 integration boundary is documented as W-phase3-independent-check.
dependency_chain_assessment.controls_sharing_common_dependency: The adequacy guard (Task 10), runtime walker (Task 7/8), and property test (Task 19) all share walk_model_schema as a common dependency. The walker floor-check (Task 10 test_walker_emits_node_for_every_top_level_field) provides a top-level root check. A shared iterator bug that drops a Sensitive node at a nested path would not be caught by the floor-check but would be caught by the property test (the missing node would mean the assertion is never run for that path — but the sensitive_nodes assert would catch a zero-nodes result). The remaining undetectable mode: walker correctly emits a Sensitive node but with wrong metadata (marker present but value_provider closure wrong). The parity test (test_walker_guard_path_sets_are_identical) catches path divergence but not value_provider correctness. The tracer-bullet integration test (Task 6) provides direct evidence that the canary value is absent from the output — this is independent of the walker's value_provider correctness and would catch a wrong closure.
dependency_chain_assessment.risk_level: Low — controls are genuinely layered; no single-point-of-failure for the complete Sensitive annotation class

notes: This is the third systems pass on the property test. Rev-3 B1 found a real filter. Rev-4 B1 found the isinstance filter introduced by the rev-3 fix. Rev-5 confirms the rev-4 rewrite closes both: the comparison is now type-agnostic at the language level with no filter surface. The remaining empty-container mode is unreachable under current MANIFEST shapes and requires a different (future) annotation pattern to become live. Recommending APPROVED_WITH_WARNINGS rather than CHANGES_REQUESTED. The blocking concern from rev-4 — 'load-bearing security property test that passes vacuously for entire known annotation classes' — is genuinely closed. Escalating the empty-container mode or any other rev-5 concern to a fourth B1 cycle would require evidence of an annotation class in the current plan scope that is affected; no such evidence exists.
